core_content.markdown:
### 3.2 Power Series 

Forfuturewcaibaiualtoferevious example, we get 



$$e^{ax}=\sum_{n\geq0}\frac{a^n}{n!}x^n $$

for every $x\in\mathbb{R}$ 1

Ta A variation of the argument used in Example 3.8 allows us to expand $f(x)=$ $(1+x)^{\alpha}$ in power series when $|x|<1$ (and for a fixed real number 添司添$\alpha\neq0)$ 1.司

To this end, given 司$\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ .and EM $n\in\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}$ , we begin by defining the generalized binomial number ${\binom{\alpha}{n}}$ by letting $\textstyle{\binom{\alpha}{0}}=1$ and, for $n\geq1$ 



$${\binom{\alpha}{n}}={\frac{\alpha(\alpha-1)(\alpha-2)\dots(\alpha-n+1)}{n!}}.$$

Lemma 3.9 Given $\alpha\in\mathbb{R}$ and $n\in\mathbb{N}$ ,we have:

$$\begin{array}{r l}&{(a)\quad{\binom{\alpha}{n}}={\binom{\alpha-1}{n}}+{\binom{\alpha-1}{n-1}}.}\\ &{(b)\quad\frac{n}{\alpha}{\binom{\alpha}{n}}={\binom{\alpha-1}{n-1}},f o r\quad e v e r y\quad\alpha\neq0.}\\ &{(c)\quad\left|{\binom{\alpha}{n}}\right|\leq1\quad w h e n e v e r\quad|\alpha|\leq1.}\end{array}$$

Proof 

(a) Is an easy computation:

$$\begin{align*}\binom{\alpha}{n}-\binom{\alpha-1}{n}&=\frac{1}{n!}\alpha(\alpha-1)(\alpha-2)\ldots(\alpha-n+1)\\&\quad-\frac{1}{n!}(\alpha-1)(\alpha-2)\ldots(\alpha-n)\\&=\frac{1}{n!}(\alpha-1)(\alpha-2)\ldots(\alpha-n+1)(\alpha-(\alpha-n))\\&=\frac{1}{(n-1)!}(\alpha-1)(\alpha-2)\ldots(\alpha-n+1)\\&=\binom{\alpha-1}{n-1}.\end{align*}$$

(b) Follows immediately from (3.9).

(c) If $|\alpha|\leq1$ , it follows from (3.9) and the triangle inequality that 

$$\left|\binom{\alpha}{n}\right|\leq\frac{|\alpha|(|\alpha|+1)(|\alpha|+2)\ldots(|\alpha|+n-1)}{n!}\leq\frac{1\cdot2\cdot\ldots\cdot n}{n!}=1.$$
